title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Rising Type 1 Trades and Falling Prices in LCFS Market; 45Z Policy Uncertainty
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:Â 

(20th January â€“ 26th January)Â Â 
  • A total of 766,013 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 31.70% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades decreased by 32.80% WoW as 1,257,513 credits were traded in CA LCFS.Â 
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $71.73/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 3.04%â€¯fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 0.29% in price, reaching $71.33/MT.Â 
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 5.98% WoW; On 28th Jan, the spot price stood at $70.25/MT. The benchmark price stood at $75.38/MT on 28th Jan.Â 
  • D3 RIN prices decreased by 0.42% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.43% WoW.Â 

cCarbonâ€™s Market ViewÂ 

The California LCFS market experienced mixed trends over the past week, with a notable increase in Type 1 trade volumes despite a decline in non-zero credit transactions. Prices saw a downward adjustment, with both Type 1 and non-zero credit prices falling week-over-week, reflecting dynamic market sentiment. Spot credit prices also declined, though benchmark prices remained higher, indicating future market expectations.Â  Feedstock prices are rising as preliminary Section 45Z guidance has temporarily restricted imported used cooking oil (UCO) eligibility for non-SAF pathways, impacting the fats, oils, and greases (FOGs) market. The Treasury Department's notice on the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Tax Credit has further shaped market expectations, with concerns over reduced demand for imported UCO. Meanwhile, Rep. Beth Van Duyne has introduced a bill to repeal the 45Z tax credit, which supports low-carbon fuel production. While some Republicans favour adjustments over repeal, farm-state lawmakers may push to retain some form of biofuel tax credit as fiscal negotiations unfold.Â Â 

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume TrendÂ 

[caption id="attachment_591720" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 3.04% WoW fall, decreasing from $73.98 to $71.73 this week, with all non-zero credit prices decreased by 0.29% WoW, falling from $71.54 to $71.33. Type 1 trades saw 766,013 credits exchanged, reflecting a 31.70% WoW increase. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades decreased by 32.80% WoW to reach 1,257,513 credits.Â 

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot PriceÂ 

[caption id="attachment_591721" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 5.98% WoW, reaching $69.15/MT. On 28th January, the average spot price was $70.25/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $75.38/MT on 28th January.Â 

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and VolumeÂ 

[caption id="attachment_591722" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 73 trades were reported in the market, with 13 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for two transactions at volumes of 5,000 and 10,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 737 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $75.00, whereas the largest transfer volume was reported for three transactions of 100,000 MT of the Type 2 category at $74.00 and Type 1 category at $71.00 and $72.50.Â 

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price TrendÂ 

[caption id="attachment_591724" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In December 2024, trading volumes surged to 7.38M, driven by year-end balance clearing, while Type 1 prices averaged $71.89. As of January 26, 2025, trading volumes have reached 5.98M, with Type 1 prices rising to an average of $72.96, indicating sustained market momentum.Â 

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price TrendÂ 

[caption id="attachment_591723" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 20th Jan â€“ 26th Jan, total VPT recorded a 0.80% decrease (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.Â 

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trendÂ 

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list.Â Â  D3 RIN prices decreased by 0.42% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 1.43% WoW.Â  D3: Cellulosic biofuelÂ  D4: Biomass-based dieselÂ  D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol)Â  D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuelÂ Â 

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this weekÂ 

  • Congresswoman Scholten Introduces Bipartisan Extension of the Section 48 ITC for Qualified Biogas Properties. Congresswoman Hillary Scholten (D-MI) and Congressman David Valadao (R-CA) introduced the Agricultural Environmental Stewardship Act of 2025 to extend the Section 48 investment tax credit (ITC) for qualified biogas properties. The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA; P.L. 117-169) extended the Sec. 48 ITC through December 31, 2024, to support the deployment of certain clean energy technologies. However, the Department of Treasuryâ€™s final rulemaking was not released until December 4, 2024â€“27 days before the expiration of the tax credit.Â Â 
  • Sen. Moran Leads Colleagues in Reintroducing Farm to Fly Act to Expand Use of Biofuels for Aviation. U.S. Senators Jerry Moran (R-Kan.), Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa), Tammy Duckworth (D-Ill.), Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.), Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) and Joni Ernst (R-Iowa) recently reintroduced legislation that would help accelerate the production and development of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) through existing U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) programs to allow further growth for alternative fuels to be used in the aviation sector and create new markets for American farmers.Â 
  • RFA Thanks President Trump for Supporting Summer E15, Continues Push for Legislation. In response to President Trumpâ€™s executive order on a national energy emergency, Renewable Fuels Association President Geoff Cooper expressed gratitude for monitoring fuel markets and potential E15 waivers but urged Congress to pass permanent legislation allowing year-round E15 sales, providing market stability and eliminating the need for emergency measures.Â
